name: Normal Lung Sounds
shortSummary: The physiological sounds made by the lungs
summary: Normal (vesicular) lung sounds are the physiological sounds that healthy lungs make during respiration. They are low-pitched and have a soft and rustling nature. The two phases are inspiration and expiration. Inspiration is longer, louder and slightly higher-pitched, while expiration is shorter, quieter and lower-pitched. There is no gap between the two phases and they are best heard over the peripheral precordium. Normal lung sounds do not rule out respiratory disease.
signs: Vesicular sounds are low-pitched
Inspiration followed by expiration with no gap
Inspiration longer than expiration
Bronchovesicular sounds (medium-pitched, with inspiration the same length as expiration) can sometimes be heard at the apices in health
However, bronchial or bronchovesicular sounds heard in the mid zones or bases suggests consolidation and are not normal

lungCases.name: Normal Lung Sounds 1
lungCases.description: Normal vesicular lung sounds with inspiration longer than expiration and no additional sounds.

lungCases.name: Normal Lung Sounds 2
lungCases.description: Normal vesicular lung sounds with inspiration longer than expiration and no additional sounds.

lungCases.name: Normal Lung Sounds 3
lungCases.description: Normal lung sounds. Note the bronchovesicular sounds at the apices, which are of medium pitch and inspiration is approximately the same length as expiration, which can be normal in health. Vesicular sounds are heard at the mid zones and bases. Bronchovesicular sounds heard at these areas would be abnormal and suggest consolidation.

xrayFindings: No specific abnormalities
medicalManagement: Remember some lung lesions do not affect respiratory sounds (e.g. cancer)
The presence of abnormal lung sounds may indicate respiratory disease, but can also occur in other diseases (e.g. crackles or wheeze in congestive cardiac failure)

name: Pulmonary oedema
searchText: Heart failure, pulmonary oedema, interstitial oedema, end inspiratory crepitations, crackles
shortSummary: Fluid accumulation in the lung's parenchyma
summary: Left-sided heart disease, including both systolic and diastolic heart failure, as well as left-sided valve disease, can cause increased left atrial pressure and hence increased pulmonary venous pressure. This causes fluid to leak out of the pulmonary capillaries into the interstitium (the connective tissue surrounding blood vessels, airways and alveoli). Initially, this squeezes closed smaller airways and alveoli, which then suddenly re-open when intra-pleural pressure is most negative at end-inspiration. This results in fine end-inspiratory crepitations, initially at the bases due to gravity. Eventually however, fluid leaks into the alveoli, which can produce coarser crepitations throughout inspiration and across all lung fields.

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pulmonary oedema causes dyspnoea due to impaired gas exchange in the lungs. Fluid in the interstitium and alveoli increases the diffusion distance from alveoli to blood. It can cause exertional dyspnoea as well as orthopnoea (breathlessness when lying flat) and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea due to gravity worsening fluid accumulation.

signs: Initially bilateral fine end-inspiratory crepitations (short, explosive sounds) at the bases to mid zones
Progressively, crepitations become coarser, start earlier in inspiration and occur higher up the lung
Crepitations sound 'wet' or bubbly as opposed to dry in pulmonary fibrosis
Expiratory wheeze (airway compression from fluid)
Associated with pleural effusions
Elevated JVP
Pedal oedema
Third heart sound

lungCases.name: Pulmonary oedema 1
lungCases.description: In addition to vesicular sounds, inspiratory crepitations are audible in the mid zones and bases. In the mid zones, the crepitations are fine and occur at the end of inspiration. At the bases however, they are coarser and start earlier in inspiration, reflecting a greater degree of fluid accumulation due to gravity.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary oedema 2
lungCases.description: In addition to vesicular sounds, inspiratory crepitations are audible in the mid zones and bases. In the mid zones, the crepitations are fine and occur at the end of inspiration. At the bases however, they are coarser and start earlier in inspiration, reflecting a greater degree of fluid accumulation due to gravity.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary oedema 3
lungCases.description: Vesicular breath sounds can be heard throughout, but there are very subtle bubbly/wet-sounding crepitations heard at the bases bilaterally (and to a much lesser extent, the mid zones). The crepitations are of medium coarseness and occur in mid-late inspiration, which is typical of pulmonary oedema. The crepitations here are subtle because this patient had been offloading with diuretics for 24 hours, and so only minimal residual interstitial/alveolar fluid remains.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary oedema 4
lungCases.description: This patient has severe pulmonary oedema. Wet-sounding crepitations, of fine-to-medium coarseness, can be heard throughout the lung fields. In addition, an expiratory wheeze can be heard at the bases, likely cardiac wheeze (airway compression from fluid).

ecgFindings: Variable but may be abnormal in LV impairment e.g. LBBB
xrayFindings: Kerley B lines
Fluid in fissures
Upper lobe venous distension
Alveolar oedema
Cardiomegaly
Pleural effusions
echoFindings: Assess LV systolic and diastolic function and presence of valve disease
medicalManagement: Diuretics if caused by volume overload/congestive cardiac failure
complications: Increased chance of secondary bacterial infections
clinicalPearls: Suspect pulmonary oedema in patients with bilateral fine end-inspiratory crepitations, especially if they have a cardiac background
Crepitations may be louder on one side of the chest
Supporting signs such as pedal oedema may be absent if there is exclusive/early left heart failure

name: Asthma
summary: Asthma describes chronic, intermittent and reversible airway obstruction. It is mediated through three main pathophysiological changes: airway inflammation and oedema, bronchospasm and excessive mucous production. Asthma is more common in developed nations (it affects around 8% of the Western population), although overall mortality is higher in low and middle-income countries due to lack of access to treatment.

causes.name: Allergic asthma
causes: Triggers: dust mites, pollen, pet dander
T-helper (Th2) cells, mast cells, eosinophils and IgE-dependent
Often less severe and responds well to steroids

causes.name: Non-allergic asthma
causes: Non-allergic triggers e.g. smoking, cold weather, pollution, exercise, NSAIDs, occupational exposure, obesity
Not dependent on Th2 cells typically, high levels of neutrophils
More severe and less responsive to steroids

symptoms.name: Wheeze
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Asthma causes intermittent episodes of wheezing due to reversible airflow limitation (bronchospasm, airway inflammation and mucous hypersecretion).

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Asthma causes intermittent episodes of shortness of breath and chest tightness, especially nocturnally, when circulating cortisol levels are lowest. Airway obstruction increases work of breathing and can lead to hyperinflation and air trapping. In severe asthma, there may be mucous plugging and hence V/Q mismatch.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Asthma typically causes a dry, intermittent cough, worse at night, when circulating cortisol levels are lowest. The cough can be productive if there is a supra-added infection. Coughing is caused by excess mucous production and irritation of cough receptors by inflammation.

signs: May be no signs outside of an exacerbation (remember asthma is intermittent)
Signs of atopy (e.g. eczema)
Prolonged expiratory phase
Bilateral polyphonic wheeze
Hyperresonant percussion note
Signs of respiratory distress in severe exacerbation (e.g. inability to complete sentences, tripod positioning, silent chest)

lungCases.name: Asthma 1
lungCases.description: This patient presented with an exacerbation of asthma. There is a prolonged expiratory phase due to air trapping and airway narrowing. A polyphonic wheeze can be heard as a continuous, high-pitched musical sound throughout expiration.

xrayFindings: Usually normal (especially during stable periods)
Can have signs of hyperinflation during exacerbations
spirometryFindings: Can be normal in stable periods
Peak-expiratory flow rate diary (>20% variation significant)
Fraction of exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) measurement > 50 ppb
Can perform bronchodilator reversibility testing (>12% improvement in FEV1 significant)
medicalManagement: Education and optimisation e.g. action-plans, trigger avoidance, smoking cessation, influenza/pneumococcal vaccines

Modern treatment regimes start with a single combination inhaler with corticosteroids and long-acting beta agonists, used as AIR (anti-inflammatory reliever) or MART (maintenance and reliever therapy)
Leukotriene receptor antagonists
Acute severe asthma: nebulised bronchodilators, oral/IV steroids, magnesium, aminophylline
complications: Airway remodelling (permanent structural changes and irreversibility)
clinicalPearls: Objective testing, including FeNO, has emerged as the preferred way to confirm the diagnosis of asthma
Guidelines have shifted away from using short-acting beta agonists alone or PRN alongside regular inhalers and instead prefer single combination inhalers, in the form of AIR/MART therapy
Metered-dose inhaler should be used with a spacer device
On hospital attendance, ask patients about nocturnal symptoms, vaccinations status and whether they have ever been admitted to ICU (increased risk of life-threatening asthma)

name: COPD
searchText: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, COPD, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, wheeze
shortSummary: Irreversible airway obstruction and alveolar destruction
summary: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is a progressive disorder of airway obstruction with little to no reversibility, associated with an inflammatory response in the lungs. It affects up to 10% of the population over 40. It has classically consisted of two pathological entities: chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Chronic 'bronchitis' is defined clinically as a chronic cough with sputum production. Despite the name 'bronchitis' the primary site of airway inflammation/narrowing, and hence obstruction, is actually the smaller airways (bronchioles), although the larger bronchi are often also inflamed and responsible for excess mucous production due to goblet cell hypertrophy. Emphysema is defined histologically/radiologically as destruction of alveoli, resulting in loss of surface area for gas exchange and loss of elastic recoil, leading to air trapping.

causes.name: Smoking
causes: Smoking also inhibits protective enzymes e.g. alpha-1 antitrypsin (emphysema)

causes.name: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
causes: Genetic variant resulting in premature emphysema with minimal/no smoking
Alpha-1 antitrypsin inhibits neutrophil elastase and other enzymes that break down alveoli

symptoms.name: Wheeze
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: COPD causes wheezing due to airway narrowing caused by a combination of bronchial mucous hypersecretion, inflammation and thickening as well as airway collapse due to loss of radial traction/elastic recoil due to alveolar destruction.

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: COPD causes shortness of breath due to a combination of airflow obstruction (from inflammation and mucous hypersecretion) and parenchymal destruction (reduced surface area for gas exchange and destruction of alveolar capillaries, hence dead-space and V/Q mismatch)

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: COPD usually causes a chronic cough, productive of sputum. It is caused by excess mucous production (goblet cell hyperplasia), reduced mucous clearance (ciliary paralysis) and airway irritation from inflammation

signs: Cachexia
Tremor: fine (from salbutamol use) or flapping (from CO2 retention)
Prolonged expiratory phase
Signs of hyperexpansion (reduced cricosternal distance, barrel chest)
Pursed lip respiration
Use of accessory muscles
Globally reduced air entry (from bullae/alveolar destruction)
Polyphonic wheeze
Hyperresonant percussion note
Cyanosis
Bounding pulse (CO2 retention)
Cor pulmonale (ankle swelling, raised JVP, loud P2)

lungCases.name: COPD 1
lungCases.description: This patient presented with an exacerbation of COPD. There is a prolonged expiratory phase due to air trapping and airway narrowing. A polyphonic wheeze can be heard as a continuous musical sound throughout the chest.

lungCases.name: COPD 2
lungCases.description: This patient has established COPD and presented with an exacerbation. Auscultation reveals a high-pitched polyphonic wheeze throughout both lung fields. The wheeze is mainly heard in mid-late expiration, likely due to successful treatment with bronchodilators, which dilate the airways, and hence there is only significant airflow limitation when the airways are most dynamically collapsed at the end of expiration. The lung sounds are also relatively quiet, especially in the mid zones, due to emphysema/destruction of airspaces.

lungCases.name: COPD 3
lungCases.description: This patient presented with an exacerbation of COPD. There is a prolonged expiratory phase due to air trapping and airway narrowing. A polyphonic wheeze can be heard as a continuous musical sound throughout the chest, alongside inspiratory and expiratory rhonchi (a low-pitched continuous snoring sound). There is reduced air entry and crepitations and the left base, possibly suggesting a pneumonia.

ecgFindings: Variable but might show right heart strain (e.g. RAD, RBBB, TWI anteriorly, P pulmonale)
xrayFindings: Hyperinflated (flattened diaphragm, >6 anterior ribs visible)
Reduced lung markings/bullae
medicalManagement: Smoking cessation
Pulmonary rehab
Influenza/pneumococcal vaccines
Inhaled bronchodilators (beta-2 agonists and muscarinic antagonists)
Inhaled corticosteroids
Rescue pack for exacerbations (steroids/abx)
Long-term oxygen therapy
clinicalPearls: On average patients with COPD get around three exacerbations per year
The chronic damage done by smoking is irreversible, but support patients to quit to prevent further injury
Classic phenotypes: blue bloater (CO2 retention, depend on hypoxic drive) vs pink puffer (CO2 normal, increased respiratory drive)

name: Pulmonary fibrosis
searchText: Pulmonary fibrosis, interstitial lung disease, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, IPF, pneumonitis, cryptogenic fibrosing alveolitis
shortSummary: Progressive thickening of the lung's interstitium
summary: Pulmonary fibrosis describes the progressive thickening and scarring of the lung's interstitium (connective tissue surrounding the alveoli and capillaries). It is the end result of many forms of interstitial lung disease (ILD). The thickened interstitium results in restrictive lung physiology, and ventilation/perfusion (V/Q) mismatch due to impaired gas exchange. The most common form if ILD is idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), where fibrosis occurs without a clear trigger. IPF affects around 50-100 per 100,000 people and has a median life expectancy of 3-5 years. Other forms of ILD can be caused by a number of environmental exposures (such as drugs and organic or inorganic dusts) as well as connective tissue disease.

causes.name: Organic dust inhalation (extrinsic allergic alveolitis, aka hypersensitivity pneumonitis) - hypersensitivity reaction to inhaled antigens.
causes: Bird fancier's lung (protein in avian faeces/feathers)
Metal fluid working (mycobacterium species in fluid metal)

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pulmonary fibrosis causes exertional dyspnoea due to impaired gas exchange in the lungs. Thickening and scarring of the interstitium and alveoli increases the diffusion distance from alveoli to blood. Additionally, restrictive lung physiology impairs ventilation.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pulmonary fibrosis causes a dry, non-productive cough, especially on exertion. As the interstitium becomes scarred and contracted, smaller airways are pulled wider open (traction bronchiectasis). This stimulates mechano-receptors, which are sensitive to airway diameter, and hence lowers the threshold for the cough reflex, such that minimal triggers (such as vibration, deep breathing or changes in air temperature) cause coughing.

signs: Cachexia
Clubbing
Fine, high-pitched, mid to late inspiratory crepitations (short, explosive sounds)
Location of crepitations depends on cause of ILD
Crepitations sound 'dry' c.f. pulmonary oedema, and are not changed by coughing
High-pitched end-inspiratory squeaks (squawks) are characteristic in extrinsic allergic alveolitis (also called 'chirping rales' due to their association with bird fancier's lung)
Cyanosis
Cor pulmonale (ankle swelling, raised JVP, loud P2)

lungCases.name: Pulmonary fibrosis 1
lungCases.description: In addition to vesicular sounds, fine, high-pitched, mid-late inspiratory crepitations are audible in the mid zones and bases. They are loudest and most prominent in the mid zones. They sound 'dry', unlike those in pulmonary oedema, which are 'wetter' and have more of a bubbling quality. This patient had established idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary fibrosis 2
lungCases.description: Fine, high-pitched, mid-late inspiratory crepitations are audible at the apices bilaterally. They sound 'dry', likely indicating apical interstitial lung disease/pulmonary fibrosis. A loud and high-pitched end-inspiratory squeak (aka a squawk) is also audible, which is a characteristic finding in extrinsic allergic alveolitis. It is caused by the opening and oscillation of small inflamed airways at the end of inspiration, reflecting inflammation of both small airways and the interstitium in interstitial lung disease.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary fibrosis 3
lungCases.description: In addition to vesicular sounds, fine, high-pitched, mid-late inspiratory crepitations are audible in the mid zones and bases. They sound 'dry', unlike those in pulmonary oedema, which are 'wetter' and have more of a bubbling quality.

ecgFindings: Variable but might show right heart strain (e.g. RAD, RBBB, TWI anteriorly)
xrayFindings: Reticular shadowing
Volume loss
Shaggy heart border
ctFindings: Ground glass opacity (haziness indicating interstitial inflammation/fluid)
Traction bronchiectasis (dilatation of airways due to being pulled by surrounding scarred interstitium
spirometryFindings: Restrictive picture (decreased FVC but normal/increased FEV1/FVC ratio > 0.7)
medicalManagement: Best supportive care (smoking cessation, pulmonary rehab influenza/pneumococcal vaccines)
Treat GORD
Novel antifibrotic therapies (e.g. nintedanib/pirfenidone) for IPF
Treatment of specific causes (e.g. cease exposure to dusts/drugs, steroids)
complications: Lung cancer (7x risk, independent of smoking)
clinicalPearls: Upper lobe predominance: ESCHART (extrinsic allergic alveolitis, sarcoidosis, coal workers pneumoconiosis, histiocytosis X, ankylosing spondylitis, radiotherapy, TB/ABPA
Lower lobe predominance: RASCO (rheumatoid arthritis, asbestosis, scleroderma, Cryotogenic fibrosing alveolitis (aka IPF), Other e.g. drugs (methotrexate, nitrofurantoin, amiodarone, bleomycin)

name: Bronchiectasis
shortSummary: Progressive widening and thickening of airways
summary: Bronchiectasis is the progressive and irreversible widening and thickening of the airways. It affects around 1 in 200 adults. It is caused by the chronic inflammatory destruction of the muscular and elastic components of airway walls, which can also mean they have a tendency to collapse. It primarily affects the structural integrity of larger airways (bronchi), impairing mucociliary clearance, hence causing chronic infections, resulting in further airway injury in a feed-forward cycle. Despite the widening of larger airways, as bronchiectasis progresses, it paradoxically causes obstructive airway physiology, as the smaller airways (which make up most of the pulmonary tree) can become narrowed or blocked by scarring or mucous. Additionally, larger airways can collapse dynamically on expiration as their walls are floppy.

causes.name: Chronic/recurrent infections
causes: Key infections: Pneumonia, pertussis (whooping cough), tuberculosis, aspergillus
Predisposing immunodeficiency: HIV, hypogammaglobulinaemia, DiGeorge syndrome

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Bronchiectasis is characterised by a chronic, daily cough, productive of copious amounts of sputum. This is caused by mucous hypersecretion and impaired mucociliary clearance, which results in mucous pooling.

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Bronchiectasis results in progressive exertional dyspnoea due to a number of mechanisms, including: obstructive airway physiology, mucous plugging, increased dead-space and loss of functional lung parenchyma.

symptoms.name: Wheeze
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Bronchiectasis causes expiratory wheeze due to airway obstruction, despite the apparent widening of larger airways. Smaller airways become scarred/blocked by mucous, and larger airways can collapse dynamically on expiration as they are floppy and lack structural support.

symptoms.name: Haemoptysis
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Bronchiectasis causes airway neovascularisation (due to chronic hypoxia and inflammation) and these newer vessels are more fragile and prone to rupture. Persistent inflammation and destruction can also directly erode vessels, causing bleeding.

signs: Clubbing
Course, low-pitched, inspiratory crepitations (short, explosive sounds), which may be more pronounced during infective exacerbations
Bilateral polyphonic wheeze
Rhonchi (low-pitched snoring like sound)
Cyanosis
Cor pulmonale (ankle swelling, raised JVP, loud P2)

lungCases.name: Bronchiectasis 1
lungCases.description: There are coarse and loud, wet-sounding crepitations throughout inspiration at the mid zones and bases bilaterally, with normal air entry, reflecting underlying bronchiectasis.

lungCases.name: Bronchiectasis 2
lungCases.description: There are coarse and loud, wet-sounding crepitations heard across all lung fields, alongside rhonchi (snoring, low-pitched continuous sounds) in both inspiration and expiration, in a patient with an exacerbation of severe underlying bronchiectasis.

ecgFindings: Variable but might show right heart strain (e.g. RAD, RBBB, TWI anteriorly)
xrayFindings: Not very sensitive and can be normal in mild disease
Generally increased lung markings and may see some signs listed below with HRCT
ctFindings: Tram-track opacities (parallel linear densities from thickened, dilated airways seen side on)
Signet ring sign (bronchial diameter larger than adjacent artery, seen in cross section)
spirometryFindings: Often obstructive picture with FEV1/FVC ratio < 0.7 in advanced disease
medicalManagement: Best supportive care (smoking cessation, pulmonary rehab, influenza/pneumococcal vaccines)
Treat exacerbations with prolonged courses of antibiotics, often 14 days when caused by Pseudomonas species
Respiratory physiotherapy and breathing techniques
Consider long-term antibiotics (antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effect)
Bronchodilators
Treat specific cause
complications: Type I or II respiratory failure
clinicalPearls: Consider HRCT, which is far more sensitive than CXR, in patients with a chronic cough with sputum production
Ask about sputum colour - a change, especially to green, may indicate an acute exacerbation

name: Pneumonia
shortSummary: Inflammation of the lung's parenchyma due to infection
summary: Pneumonia describes inflammation of the lung's parenchyma due to an infectious agent. It results in fluid and pus accumulation in the alveoli (consolidation) and can cause impaired gas exchange, as these alveoli continue to receive perfusion with blood, but no ventilation with air (V/Q mismatch). Young children, the elderly and those who are immunocompromised are the most vulnerable. 85% of pneumonia deaths occur in those ≥ 70 years.

causes.name: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
causes: Presents before or within 48 h of hospital attendance
Typical bacteria: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, S. aureus, M. catarrhalis
Atypical bacteria: M. pneumoniae, L. pneumophilia, Chlamydophila species

causes.name: Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP)
causes: Presents after 48 h of hospital attendance
Risk factors: Extremes of age, ventilator (VAP), underlying lung disease, tracheostomy
Organisms: Gram negative bacilli (e.g. E.coli, Pseudomonas species, Klebsiella species), S. aureus

causes.name: Aspiration pneumonia
causes: Impaired swallow reflex: stroke, bulbar palsies, decreased consciousness (e.g. post-ictal)
Bugs usually mixed, including anaerobes from the mouth flora

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Lobar pneumonia causes alveolar consolidation, which stimulates cough receptors due to both the mechanical pressure on the alveolar lining, and inflammatory cytokines in the mucous. Coughing works to expel this mucous from the alveoli, towards the larger airways. Bacterial pneumonia usually causes a productive (sputum-producing) cough, while viral/atypical pneumonia usually produces a dry cough, as there is mostly inflammation of the interstitium rather than pus in the alveoli.

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pneumonia, as with any significant infection, causes the release of pyrogens, such as IL-1 and TNF-α, which travel in the bloodstream to the hypothalamus, causing it to increase the body's target temperature. Fever is a defence mechanism that reduces the ability of pathogens to reproduce as effectively.

symptoms.name: Chest pain
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Lobar pneumonia can result in sharp, pleuritic chest pain (worse on deep inspiration, coughing or sneezing). This is mainly due to inflammation of adjacent parietal pleural (which has many pain fibres, unlike the lung's parenchyma).

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pneumonia causes dyspnoea due to impaired gas exchange - consolidated alveoli are no longer ventilated with air, but continue to receive blood supply (hence V/Q mismatch).

symptoms.name: Haemoptysis
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Haemoptysis can occur in pneumonia as alveolar capillaries in the affected area are made more permeable and hence allow red blood cell extravasation into the alveolar spaces (red hepatization). These red cells can then be coughed up. Additionally, inflammation and certain virulence factors can rupture superficial blood vessels.

signs: Fever
Tachycardia/tachypnoea
Herpes labialis reactivation (especially in S. pneumoniae)
Coarse, low-pitched early-mid inspiratory crepitations (short, explosive sounds)
Bronchial breathing (harsh, high-pitched breath sounds, where the expiratory phase is as long as the inspiratory phase, due to consolidation increasing sound conduction from larger airways
Diminished breath sounds and dullness to percussion (especially if associated with a pleural effusion)
Pleural rub (grating sound from inflamed pleural surfaces rubbing together)
Increased vocal resonance/tactile vocal fremitus

lungCases.name: Pneumonia 1
lungCases.description: There are coarse crepitations heard at the right mid zone and base, reflecting consolidation from a lobar pneumonia. Bronchial breath sounds can be heard at the right mid zone due to increased transmission to the chest wall from central airways, while reduced air entry at the right base indicates dense consolidation/collapse.

lungCases.name: Pneumonia 2
lungCases.description: There are coarse crepitations and bronchial breath sounds heard at the left mid zone and base, reflecting consolidation from a lobar pneumonia.

lungCases.name: Pneumonia 3
lungCases.description: There are coarse crepitations and bronchial breathing (higher-pitched, hollow sounds where expiration is excessive loud and prolonged) heard at the left mid zone and base, reflecting consolidation from a lobar pneumonia. Heart sounds can be heard radiating throughout the chest as well.

lungCases.name: Pneumonia 4
lungCases.description: This patient has an aspiration pneumonia in the right mid-lower zones. Coarse inspiratory crepitations can be heard in these regions, alongside an expiratory scratching/grating pleural rub, due to resultant pleural inflammation.

xrayFindings: Lobar consolidation (homogenous opacity in one lobe, air bronchograms, silhouette sign)
Bronchopneumonia (ill-defined opacities, multiple lobes)
Interstitial infiltrates bilaterally e.g. atypical or viral infection)
medicalManagement: Antibiotics
Supportive care
Follow up CXR in 6-8 weeks if abnormal (check resolution)
clinicalPearls: Use the CURB-65 score to help grade severity and determine need for admission
Consider atypical presentations and atypical infections (especially in the elderly or immunocompromised) e.g. general weakness, altered mental status

name: Pleural effusion
shortSummary: Fluid accumulation in the pleural space
summary: A pleural effusion is the accumulation of fluid in the pleural space (between the parietal and visceral pleura). Normally, there is a small amount of serous pleural fluid (10-20 ml) in the pleural space, to allow the smooth motion of the lungs during respiration.

causes.name: Transudative effusion (increased capillary filtration pressure)
causes: Increased hydrostatic pressure e.g. L or R heart failure, valve disease, renal failure
Decreased plasma oncotic pressure e.g. cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, systemic illness, protein-losing enteropathy

causes.name: Exudative effusion (inflammation causing increased capillary permeability)
causes: Malignancy e.g. primary (bronchogenic carcinoma or mesothelioma) or secondary (e.g. metastasis)

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pleural effusions cause shortness of breath due to a combination of restrictive ventilation/decreased compliance and diaphragmatic splinting, as well as localised atelectasis, resulting in shunting of blood through non-aerated lung segments, hence V/Q mismatch.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pleural effusions often cause a dry, non-productive cough due to both direct stretch and/or inflammation causing pleural irritation and stimulating cough receptors.

symptoms.name: Chest pain
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Unlike the visceral pleura, the parietal pleura is innervated by sensory pain receptors (from intercostal nerves), so inflammation can cause sharp pleuritic pain, which can radiate to the neck (due to diaphragmatic irritation stimulating the phrenic nerve). Paradoxically, as fluid accumulates, the pain may reduce as the pleural space is lubricated, which prevents the two layers from rubbing together. Transudative effusions rarely cause significant pain, as the parietal pleura is stretched but not inflamed.

signs: May be signs of the cause (e.g. cachexia or clubbing in malignancy)
Decreased expansion of affected side
Deviation of the trachea away from side of effusion
Stony dullness to percussion
Decreased air entry over effusion
Decreased tactile vocal fremitus/vocal resonance over effusion, but may get egophony and bronchial breathing above effusion due to compressed/atelectatic lung

lungCases.name: Pleural effusion 1
lungCases.description: This patient has a large unilateral left-sided pleural effusion, reaching to the mid zone. Air entry is significantly reduced over the effusion. Bronchial breath sounds can be heard at the upper border of the effusion, at the left apex, due to compression and collapse of the lung parenchyma here. Bronchial breath sounds are hollow and high-pitched, and expiration is at least as loud and prolonged as inspiration (unlike vesicular sounds, where the inspiratory component is louder and longer). Additionally, there is often a gap between the inspiratory and expiratory sound. It is caused by the increased transmission of sounds from the central airways/trachea, by collapsed or consolidated lung (solids are better sound transmitters than air).

lungCases.name: Pleural effusion 1
lungCases.description: This patient has a moderate unilateral right-sided pleural effusion at the base. Air entry is significantly reduced over the effusion. Medium-coarseness crepitations can be heard at the right mid zone, either indicating compressive atelectasis or an underlying pneumonia.

lungCases.name: Pleural effusion 3
lungCases.description: There is decreased air entry at both bases due to bilateral moderate pleural effusions.

xrayFindings: Usually at least 200 ml to be visible on AP CXR
Initially blunting of the costophrenic angles
Then dense opacity without lung markers and meniscus sign (higher at lateral edges than centre)
Massive effusions (>1000 ml) cause mediastinal shifts to the contralateral side
ctFindings: Can assess for cause/malignancy (best once drained)
bloodTestFindings: For comparison with pleural fluid sample
Light's criteria - exudative if any of: pleural protein to serum protein > 0.5, pleural LDH to serum LDH > 0.6, pleural LDH > 0.66 of ULN serum LDH reference range
microbiologyFindings: Pleural fluid aspirate can be sent for MCS and AFB/TB culture
medicalManagement: Treat underlying cause
Diuretics if heart failure/transudative
Antibiotics if infective
percutaneousManagement: Percutaneous aspiration ± chest drain indicated if: respiratory compromise, suspected empyema, needing diagnosis/cytology
Ultrasound-guided if inserted using Seldinger technique
surgicalManagement: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) for complications e.g. loculated/non-resolving effusion, recurrent effusions, decortication
complications: Pleural thickening and scarring (restrictive lung defect)
clinicalPearls: Pleural acidosis (pH < 7.2) is indicative of empyema
Effusion from heart failure are usually bilateral, but can be unilateral (R > L)
Be careful when draining large effusions - clamp after ≈ 1.5 L (risk of re-expansion pulmonary oedema)

name: Pneumothorax
shortSummary: Air accumulation in the pleural space
summary: A pneumothorax is the accumulation of air in the pleural space, due to air leak, either from the lung (visceral pleura) or through the chest wall (parietal pleura).

causes.name: Primary
causes: Often in young, tall, thin men (rupture of subpleural bullae)
Increased risk with smoking (100x lifetime risk)

causes.name: Secondary
causes: Chronic lung disease (e.g. asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis
Connective tissue disease (e.g. Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome

causes.name: Traumatic
causes: Blunt/penetrating injury to the chest wall

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pneumothoraces cause shortness of breath due to a combination of restrictive ventilation/decreased compliance (causing increased work of breathing), as well as atelectasis of underlying lung, resulting in shunting of blood through non-aerated lung segments, hence V/Q mismatch. Tension pneumothoraces cause mediastinal and tracheal shift and then haemodynamic collapse.

symptoms.name: Chest pain
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pneumothoraces cause acute, sharp, pleuritic chest pain. Air leak (± other material from the lung) cause acute mechanical (± chemical) irritation of the parietal pleura, which is highly innervated by sensory pain receptors from intercostal nerves (adjacent to the chest wall) and the phrenic nerve (over the diaphragm).

signs: Reduced expansion
Hyperresonant percussion note
Decreased breath sounds
Decreased tactile vocal fremitus/vocal resonance
Tension pneumothorax: tracheal deviation to opposite side, hypotension, respiratory and circulatory collapse

lungCases.name: Pneumothorax 1
lungCases.description: This patient a tension pneumothorax on the left side. There is minimal air entry on the left and the patient is severely tachypnoeic and in respiratory distress.

lungCases.name: Pneumothorax 2
lungCases.description: There is reduced air entry at the right apex due to a right apical pneumothorax.

xrayFindings: Radiolucent area in the pleural space without lung markers and defined visceral pleural line
Often appear at apices on erect CXRs
Tension: mediastinal shift, depressed diaphragm
Deep sulcus sign if supine
medicalManagement: For small and/or asymptomatic pneumothoraces, conservative management by monitoring may be all that is required
The use of oxygen may increase the speed of resolution
Smoking cessation
Cannot fly for 2 weeks after resolution
Never scuba dive again (unless had definitive surgical management e.g. pleurectomy/pleurodesis)
percutaneousManagement: Symptomatic and/or secondary pneumothoraces usually need a chest drain
Urgent needle decompression for tension pneumothorax
surgicalManagement: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) for recurrent pneumothoraces e.g. pleurodesis
complications: Tension pneumothorax, respiratory failure and cardiac arrest
clinicalPearls: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis - clinical examination is key (so don't delay needle decompression)
Classical CXR signs may be absent in supine patients (may only have deep sulcus sign)
The absence of lung sliding on ultrasound is a useful additive diagnostic sign

name: Pleural rub
shortSummary: A grating sound occurring in pleuritis
summary: A pleural rub is an abnormal grating, scratching or creaking sound that occurs when inflamed visceral and parietal pleural layers move over one another. Detection of a pleural rub requires evaluation to determine the underlying cause.

causes.name: Infectious
causes: Lobar pneumonia (4% of cases, adjacent pleura inflamed)
Viral infections causing pleurisy (e.g. influenza, coxsackievirus)

symptoms.name: Chest pain
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Pleural rubs are often associated with pleurisy/pleuritic chest pain (sharp, stabbing pain worse on inspiration, coughing or sneezing), due to the underlying driver of pleural inflammation.

signs: Grating, scratching or creaking sound occurring as the the inflamed pleural layers slide over one another
Sounds like 'walking on fresh snow' or 'leather rubbing against leather'
Often biphasic (in both inspiration or expiration), but may be only heard in one phase of the respiratory cycle
Usually localised to the area where the pleura is inflamed
Does not clear with coughing
Sound may disappear if pleural effusion develops (as friction reduced and pleural layers separated from one another)
May be associated with crepitations if underlying pneumonic process

lungCases.name: Pleural rub 1
lungCases.description: This patient presented with pleuritic chest pain and was diagnosed with a left lower lobe segmental pulmonary embolism. A scratching/grating pleural rub can be heard in the left mid zone and base, due to resultant pleural inflammation. There are no underlying crepitations and there is no significant parenchymal process.

lungCases.name: Pleural rub 2
lungCases.description: This patient has an aspiration pneumonia in the right mid-lower zones. Coarse inspiratory crepitations can be heard in these regions, alongside an expiratory scratching/grating pleural rub, due to resultant pleural inflammation.

medicalManagement: Treatment of underlying cause or pleural inflammation
Analgesia
clinicalPearls: A pleural rub sounds similar to a pericardial friction rub, but can be differentiated by its timing around the respiratory cycle (e.g. get the patient to hold their breath - a pleural rub will stop, unlike a pericardial friction rub)
Once detected, hunt for the underlying cause

name: Pulmonary resection
searchText: Surgical resection, lobectomy, pneumonectomy, segmentectomy, wedge,
summary: The lung's parenchyma can be surgical resected for lung cancer, or more rarely other diseases. Options include: a wedge resection (removing a small triangular piece of lung tissue), a segmentectomy (removing a larger anatomical segment of a lobe), a lobectomy (removing an entire lobe - there are 3 lobes in the right lung and 2 in the left) or pneumonectomy (removing an entire lung). Depending on the extent of resection, there may be signs on examination.

causes.name: Lung cancer
causes: Usually early-stage, non-small cell lung cancer
The most common reason for lobectomy
Central or larger tumours that have invaded the main bronchus need a pneumonectomy.

causes.name: Chronic infections
causes: A rare indication in modern practice
Includes: TB, aspergilloma, persistent lung abscesses

causes.name: Localised chronic lung disease
causes: E.g. in COPD as part of lung volume reduction surgery

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Large volume resection, such as post-pneumonectomy, can cause long-term shortness of breath on exertion, due to the reduction in total lung capacity.

signs: Smaller volume resections are often performed minimally invasively via VATS (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery) so there may only be 2-4 small 'keyhole' incisions on the lateral chest wall
Patients who have undergone pneumonectomy will have a unilateral thoracotomy scar
Post wedge resection, segmentectomy or lobectomy, there may be no other signs on examination as the remaining lung expands to take the place of the resected lung

Post-pneumonectomy, the space is eventually filled with a serous fluid, so there is usually ipsilateral severely reduced/absent air entry, dullness to percussion and tracheal shift. The contralateral lung expands to help fill the space.

lungCases.name: Pulmonary resection 1
lungCases.description: This patient has undergone a right pneumonectomy for lung cancer. There is significantly reduced/absent air entry over the entire right hemithorax (the sounds heard are transmitted from the left hemithorax).

lungCases.name: Pulmonary resection 2
lungCases.description: This patient has undergone a left-upper lobectomy. The lung sounds are essentially normal, other than being very subtly reduced, because the left lower lobe has expanded to take the place of the excised lobe. Note bronchovesicular sounds are heard at the apices, which can be normal at these positions.

xrayFindings: Smaller resections may show no obvious signs
Post-pneumonectomy, the space is filled with a dense serous fluid, with mediastinal shift over to the side of the resection and a raised hemidiaphragm
spirometryFindings: Lobectomy may only minimally reduce spirometry values (e.g. by <10%)
Pneumonectomy usually reduces values by less than 50% as there is compensation by the remaining lung e.g. FEV1 reduced by 25-40% ad FVC by 30-40%
complications: Acute complications: persistent air leak, pneumonia, atelectasis and post-op atrial fibrillation

Long-term complications: reduced lung function, bronchopleural fistula, post-pneumonectomy syndrome (rare late complication where shift of mediastinal structures causes compression of trachea), cardiac herniation, chronic pain
clinicalPearls: Pre-operative lung function needs to be adequate to allow surgical resection, usually FEV1 >1.5 L for lobectomy and  FEV1 >2 L for pneumonectomy.
Remember that many patients may have no signs other than scars

name: Atelectasis
shortSummary: Partial collapse of the lung's parenchyma
summary: Atelectasis describes the partial collapse of the lung's parenchyma. It can either be used to refer to collapse of small segments of the lung or collapse of an entire lobe ('lobar collapse'). Alveolar collapse leads to impaired gas exchange and compromised respiratory function.

causes.name: Obstructive/resorptive
causes: Airway obstruction prevents ventilation of a section of lung and the distal air is resorbed into the circulation
Causes: mucous plugs, foreign bodies, tumours
High FiO2 can contribute as oxygen is more easily resorbed than nitrogen

causes.name: Compressive
causes: Increased (i.e. less negative) pressure in pleural space causes alveolar collapse
Causes: pleural effusion, pneumothorax, hiatus hernia, raised hemidiaphragm

causes.name: Adhesive
causes: Causes: acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and neonatal respiratory distress syndrome

causes.name: Passive
causes: Reduced tidal volume (shallow breathing) or bedridden supine patients/gravity dependent changes in lung volume

causes.name: Post-operative
causes: Mix of above mechanisms, including: reduced tidal volume due to pain or narcosis from analgesic or anaesthetic agents, supine positioning

symptoms.name: Dyspnoea
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Atelectasis causes dyspnoea due to impaired gas exchange - collapsed alveoli are no longer ventilated with air, but continue to receive blood supply (hence V/Q mismatch).

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.valveSymptomDescription: Atelectasis was previously cited as a common reason for a low-grade fever in the initial post-operative period, but modern evidence shows no link, and is likely due to an inflammatory response from the surgery itself.

signs: Patient often supine with reduced respiratory effort
Decreased air entry (especially bibasally)
Fine, late-inspiratory crepitations, which clear with coughing
Decreased chest expansion

lungCases.name: Atelectasis 1
lungCases.description: This patient is recovering from general anaesthesia post abdominal surgery. The respiratory rate and effort are reduced due to opioid-based analgesia, resulting in bibasal atelectasis. Reduced breath sounds and fine crepitations can be appreciated at the bases.

xrayFindings: Opacification as collapsed lung is denser than aerated lung
Segmental collapse produce linear, horizontal, 'plate-like' opacities

Lobar collapse produces characteristic signs e.g. lower lobe collapse causes triangular opaque shadows = sail-sign, LUL collapse causes 'veil-like' opacification of left hemithorax, RUL collapse causes superior displacement of the horizontal fissure
Indirect signs of volume loss (e.g. diaphragmatic elevation, mediastinal shift, rib crowding
medicalManagement: Conservative e.g. upright positioning, early mobilisation post-op, incentive spirometry, minimising use of opioid analgesia
Mucolytics e.g. acetylcysteine orally or saline nebulisers
percutaneousManagement: Bronchoscopy in selected cases for removal of impacted mucous plugs
clinicalPearls: Ensure good mobilisation and upright positioning (chair vs bed) to prevent atelectasis
Crepitations from atelectasis often resolve on coughing, unlike those in pneumonia or pulmonary oedema
